cs153.title: Stanford CS 153 Lecture Notes
cs153.summary: Notes and reflections from Stanford's CS 153 ("AI Coachella"), a tour of the frontier-AI stack told by the people building it, written up lecture by lecture.

harness-engineering.title: Harness Engineering
harness-engineering.summary: Notes and write-ups on building agent harnesses: orchestration, tools, evals, and the engineering around LLM systems.

system-design.title: System Design
system-design.summary: Notes on designing systems at scale: architecture, trade-offs, reliability, and the patterns behind them.

posts.title: CS 153, Lecture 1: When the Security Chief Becomes the Defendant
posts.summary: Joe Sullivan ran security at Facebook, Uber, and Cloudflare, and became the first CISO criminally prosecuted in the US. His lecture is about liability, transparency, resilience, and a threat landscape that AI is rewriting in real time.

posts.title: CS 153, Lecture 2: Why a Gigawatt Isn't a Gigawatt
posts.summary: Amin Vahdat runs Google's infrastructure. His lecture reframed the compute race for me: the metric that matters is value per dollar, not gigawatts, and the hard problems are system balance, reliability, and energy.

posts.title: CS 153, Lecture 3: Boil the Ocean (YC on the Agentic Company)
posts.summary: Garry Tan and Diana Hu from Y Combinator on the software factory, the closed-loop company, and why the old instinct to 'not boil the ocean' is now wrong. The part that matters most: taste, and evals, are the moat.

posts.title: CS 153, Lecture 4: Energy, Nuclear Power, and the Uranium Bottleneck
posts.summary: Scott Nolan of General Matter argues that energy, and specifically domestic uranium enrichment, is the upstream constraint on AI scaling, applying a SpaceX-style first-principles playbook to rebuild US nuclear fuel capacity.

posts.title: CS 153, Lecture 5: Reinventing Computing for the AI Era
posts.summary: Jensen Huang argues computing is being reinvented for the first time in 60 years, covering NVIDIA's co-design speedups, the generational architecture roadmap, the MFU debate, open models, energy demand, and export controls.

posts.title: CS 153, Lecture 6: Leadership, Culture, and Reinventing Venture Capital
posts.summary: Ben Horowitz of a16z on building a scalable VC firm, why culture is a set of actions rather than beliefs, why companies need decisive leadership, and how AI has changed the economics of startups and moats.

posts.title: CS 153, Lecture 7: The Evolution of Product Management in the AI Era
posts.summary: Nicole Singhal maps company growth into four phases of product management and argues that AI is merging the PM, designer, and engineer roles into a single hands-on 'product builder' rather than killing the function.

posts.title: CS 153, Lecture 8: Unified Multimodal AI and the Luma Video Factory
posts.summary: Amit Jain of Luma AI on why generative AI should be designed around where data lives, how unified single-backbone transformers beat federated pipelines for multimodal intelligence, and what the shift means for the creative industry.

posts.title: CS 153, Lecture 9: Visual Intelligence and the Frontier of Generative Modeling
posts.summary: Andreas Blattmann of Black Forest Labs on natural versus unnatural representations, latent diffusion, the diffusion versus autoregressive trade-off, and how a 25-person team bootstrapped a frontier visual AI company with Flux.

posts.title: CS 153, Lecture 10: Frontier Audio AI and the Future of Voice
posts.summary: ElevenLabs CEO Mati Staniszewski on building frontier audio AI, from the Polish dubbing origin story to the cascaded versus fused architecture debate, value-based pricing, voice safety, and a five-year platform vision.

posts.title: CS 153, Lecture 11: The Frontier AI Stack, Context, and Compute
posts.summary: Anjney Midha opens the quarter with a map of the whole AI stack, the case that context and verifiable feedback loops decide where progress and value go, and a hard look at why compute isn't a commodity.

posts.title: AI and Labor Markets: The Implementation Gap and Displacement
posts.summary: A panel on how AI reshapes labor markets, contrasting aggressive automation timelines with the implementation gap, an aging-driven demographic counterweight, and the entry-level displacement signal showing up in payroll data.
